content:
# Leviticus Chapter 25 — SOPHIA Consensus Translation

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Sources:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)
**Hebrew Base:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 73r–73v
**LXX Reference:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 111–112
**Disagreement Log:** `disagreements/LEV_025.md`

---

## Council Notes

Leviticus 25 is the theological and economic summit of the Holiness Code. The chapter is structured concentrically around the axiomatic declaration at its center: *ki-li ha-aretz* — "for the land is mine" (v. 23). From this single claim the entire edifice radiates: because the land belongs to the LORD, it cannot be permanently alienated from its original families (vv. 23–34); because Israelites are the LORD's servants, they cannot be permanently enslaved to any human master (vv. 39–55). The Jubilee is thus not merely an economic mechanism but a periodic liturgical re-enactment of Exodus theology and creation order, pushing the sabbatical principle (day, year, seven-times-seven year) to its logical extreme.

The chapter divides into six sections: (1) the sabbatical year for the land (vv. 1–7); (2) the Jubilee proclamation and its commercial implications (vv. 8–22); (3) the theological foundation and land-redemption law (vv. 23–34); (4) the care of the impoverished brother and the prohibition on usury (vv. 35–38); (5) the law against permanent Israelite enslavement by Israelites (vv. 39–46); (6) the law of redemption for an Israelite enslaved to a foreigner (vv. 47–55).

The chapter closes with a *ki-li* declaration (v. 55) that mirrors v. 23: as the land is mine, so the people are mine. Both claims together constitute the chapter's complete theology: divine ownership of land and people is the ground of economic justice and human dignity.

**Key term rulings:** YHWH = "the LORD" | bney yisrael = "sons of Israel" | yovel = "Jubilee" | deror = "liberty" | go'el = "kinsman-redeemer" (or "redeemer") | ge'ulah = "redemption" | ger = "sojourner" | toshav = "settler" (or "resident alien" in paired constructions) | sakhir = "hired worker" | neshekh = "interest" | tarbit = "profit" | be-farekh = "ruthlessly" | shabbat shabbaton = "Sabbath of complete rest" | sefiach = "what grows of itself" | achuzah = "property/holding" | mishpachah = "clan" | mot yumat formulas not applicable | "sons of Israel" throughout

---

## Consensus Translation

**25:1** And the LORD spoke to Moses on Mount Sinai, saying:

**25:2** "Speak to the sons of Israel and say to them: When you come into the land that I am giving you, the land shall keep a Sabbath to the LORD.

**25:3** For six years you shall sow your field, and for six years you shall prune your vineyard and gather in its produce,

**25:4** but the seventh year shall be a Sabbath of complete rest for the land, a Sabbath to the LORD. You shall not sow your field or prune your vineyard.

**25:5** What grows of itself from your harvest you shall not reap, and the grapes of your untended vine you shall not gather. It shall be a year of complete rest for the land.

**25:6** The Sabbath of the land shall be food for you: for you and for your male servant and for your female servant and for your hired worker and for the sojourner who lives with you,

**25:7** and for your livestock and for the wild animals that are in your land: all its yield shall be for food.

**25:8** "And you shall count for yourself seven weeks of years, seven times seven years, so that the time of the seven weeks of years shall give you forty-nine years.

**25:9** Then you shall sound the ram's horn blast on the tenth day of the seventh month. On the Day of Atonement you shall sound the ram's horn throughout all your land.

**25:10** And you shall consecrate the fiftieth year, and proclaim liberty throughout the land to all its inhabitants. It shall be a Jubilee for you, when each of you shall return to his property and each of you shall return to his clan.

**25:11** That fiftieth year shall be a Jubilee for you. In it you shall neither sow nor reap what grows of itself nor gather from the untended vines.

**25:12** For it is a Jubilee. It shall be holy to you. You may eat the produce of the field.

**25:13** "In this year of Jubilee each of you shall return to his property.

**25:14** And when you sell anything to your neighbor or buy from your neighbor, you shall not wrong one another.

**25:15** According to the number of years after the Jubilee you shall buy from your neighbor, and according to the number of years for crops he shall sell to you.

**25:16** If the years are many, you shall increase the price, and if the years are few, you shall reduce the price, for it is the number of harvests he is selling to you.

**25:17** You shall not wrong one another, but you shall fear your God, for I am the LORD your God.

**25:18** "And you shall do my statutes and keep my rules and do them, and you will dwell in the land securely.

**25:19** The land will yield its fruit, and you will eat your fill and dwell in it securely.

**25:20** And if you say, 'What shall we eat in the seventh year, if we may not sow or gather in our crop?'

**25:21** I will command my blessing on you in the sixth year, so that it will produce a crop sufficient for three years.

**25:22** And when you sow in the eighth year, you will be eating from the old crop; until the ninth year, when its crop arrives, you shall eat the old.

**25:23** "And the land shall not be sold in perpetuity, for the land is mine. For you are sojourners and settlers with me.

**25:24** And throughout all the land of your holding you shall provide for the redemption of the land.

**25:25** "If your brother becomes poor and sells part of his property, then his nearest kinsman-redeemer shall come and redeem what his brother has sold.

**25:26** If a man has no one to redeem it, and he himself becomes prosperous and finds sufficient means to redeem it,

**25:27** let him calculate the years since its sale and repay the balance to the man to whom he sold it, and so he shall return to his property.

**25:28** But if he does not find sufficient means to restore it to him, then what he sold shall remain in the hand of the buyer until the year of Jubilee. In the Jubilee it shall be released, and he shall return to his property.

**25:29** "If a man sells a dwelling house in a walled city, he may redeem it within a full year after its sale. For a full year he shall have the right of redemption.

**25:30** If it is not redeemed before the completion of a full year, then the house in the walled city shall pass in perpetuity to the buyer throughout his generations; it shall not be released in the Jubilee.

**25:31** But houses in villages that have no surrounding wall shall be classified with the open fields of the land. They may be redeemed, and they shall be released in the Jubilee.

**25:32** "As for the cities of the Levites, the Levites shall have a permanent right of redemption for the houses in the cities of their holding.

**25:33** And if one of the Levites redeems a house, then the house that was sold in a city of their holding shall be released in the Jubilee. For the houses in the cities of the Levites are their holding among the sons of Israel.

**25:34** But the open land belonging to their cities may not be sold, for that is their permanent holding.

**25:35** "If your brother becomes poor and cannot support himself with you, you shall support him as a sojourner and a settler, and he shall live with you.

**25:36** Take no interest or profit from him, but fear your God, that your brother may live beside you.

**25:37** You shall not lend him your money at interest, nor give him your food for profit.

**25:38** I am the LORD your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt to give you the land of Canaan, and to be your God.

**25:39** "If your brother becomes poor beside you and sells himself to you, you shall not make him serve as a slave.

**25:40** He shall be with you as a hired worker and as a settler. He shall serve with you until the year of the Jubilee.

**25:41** Then he shall go out from you, he and his children with him, and return to his own clan and to the property of his fathers.

**25:42** For they are my servants whom I brought out of the land of Egypt; they shall not be sold as slaves.

**25:43** You shall not rule over him ruthlessly, but you shall fear your God.

**25:44** As for your male slave and your female slave whom you may have: you may buy male and female slaves from among the nations that are around you.

**25:45** You may also buy from among the sons of the settlers who are temporary residents among you and from their families that are with you, who have been born in your land, and they may become your property.

**25:46** You may bequeath them to your sons after you to inherit as a permanent holding. You may make slaves of them, but over your brothers the sons of Israel you shall not rule, one over another, ruthlessly.

**25:47** "If a sojourner or settler with you becomes prosperous, and your brother beside him becomes poor and sells himself to the sojourner or settler with you or to a member of the sojourner's clan,

**25:48** then after he is sold he retains the right of redemption. One of his brothers may redeem him,

**25:49** or his uncle or his uncle's son may redeem him, or a near relative from his clan may redeem him. Or if he grows prosperous enough, he may redeem himself.

**25:50** He shall calculate with his buyer from the year when he sold himself to him until the year of Jubilee, and the price of his release shall be based on the number of years. The time he was with his buyer shall be reckoned as the time of a hired worker.

**25:51** If there are still many years remaining, in proportion to them he shall return his redemption price from the sum he was purchased for.

**25:52** And if few years remain until the year of Jubilee, he shall calculate and pay for his redemption in proportion to his years of service.

**25:53** He shall be with him as a hired worker year by year. He shall not rule over him ruthlessly in your sight.

**25:54** And if he is not redeemed by these means, then he and his children shall go out in the year of Jubilee.

**25:55** For it is to me that the sons of Israel are servants. They are my servants whom I brought out of the land of Egypt. I am the LORD your God."

---

## Verse-by-Verse Ruling Notes

**V. 1 — Address formula.** EMET: "spoke to Moshe on Mount Sinai." LOGOS: "spoke to Moses on Mount Sinai, saying." PNEUMA: "spoke to Moses on Mount Sinai" (no "saying"). Consensus: LOGOS — "spoke to Moses on Mount Sinai, saying." English name forms per project convention. EMET's "Moshe" not adopted. The "saying" (*lemor*) is present in the MT and retained.

**V. 2 — "The land shall keep a Sabbath."** EMET: "the land shall sabbath a sabbath to the LORD." LOGOS: "the land shall keep a Sabbath to the LORD." PNEUMA: "the land itself must observe a Sabbath rest." Consensus: LOGOS — "the land shall keep a Sabbath to the LORD." EMET's double-sabbath is woodenly literal. PNEUMA's "must observe" introduces an obligation-modal not in the MT at this point; the MT's verbal construction (*ve-shavetah ha-aretz shabbat*) is an indicative statement about what the land shall do. LOGOS follows the MT's grammar correctly.

**V. 4 — "Sabbath of complete rest" vs. "Sabbath of solemn rest."** EMET: "a Sabbath of complete rest." LOGOS: "a Sabbath of solemn rest." PNEUMA: "a complete Sabbath rest." Consensus: "a Sabbath of complete rest" — following EMET and the intensified doubled construction *shabbat shabbaton*. "Complete rest" captures the superlative force more directly than "solemn rest," and PNEUMA's reordering loses the genitive construction. The same formula appears in vv. 4 and 5 and both are rendered identically.

**V. 5 — "Untended vine" vs. "undressed vine."** EMET: "your consecrated vine" (preserving the nazir connection). LOGOS: "undressed vine." PNEUMA: "untended vines." Consensus: "untended vine" — PNEUMA. The Hebrew *nezireikhah* (from *nazir*, "separated/consecrated") describes a vine left unpruned, and while EMET's "consecrated vine" preserves the cognate link with the Nazirite, it risks confusion. "Undressed" (LOGOS) is traditional but archaic. "Untended" (PNEUMA) most naturally conveys the agricultural reality to modern readers without distorting the meaning. Applied consistently to vv. 5 and 11.

**V. 6 — "Sabbath of the land shall be food for you."** All three agents agree on the substance. EMET: "the Sabbath of the land shall be food for you." LOGOS: same. PNEUMA: "whatever the land produces during its Sabbath year, you... may eat it." Consensus: LOGOS — "The Sabbath of the land shall be food for you." PNEUMA's paraphrase, while clear, restructures the Hebrew syntax substantially. The MT opens with *ve-hayetah shabbat ha-aretz lakhem le-okhlah* — "and the Sabbath of the land shall be for you for food" — which LOGOS renders faithfully.

**V. 7 — "Wild animals."** EMET: "wild animals [chayyat ha-aretz]." LOGOS: "wild animals." PNEUMA: "wild animals." All agree. Consensus: "wild animals."

**V. 8 — "Count for yourself" vs. "you shall count."** EMET: "And you shall count for yourself." LOGOS: "You shall count seven weeks of years for yourself." PNEUMA: "Count off seven Sabbath-years." Consensus: "And you shall count for yourself" — with LOGOS's "seven weeks of years" appended: "And you shall count for yourself seven weeks of years." The *lekha* ("for yourself") is in the MT and preserved per EMET/LOGOS. PNEUMA drops it and uses "Sabbath-years" rather than "weeks of years" (*shabbatot shanim*). The *vav* opening (*ve-safarta*) is retained.

**V. 9 — "Ram's horn blast" vs. "loud trumpet."** EMET: "ram's horn blast" (*shofar teru'ah*). LOGOS: "loud trumpet." PNEUMA: "ram's horn." Consensus: "ram's horn blast" — EMET/PNEUMA. The Hebrew *shofar* is specifically a ram's horn, not a metal trumpet. LOGOS's "loud trumpet" follows a formal tradition (ESV) but *shofar* should not be rendered as "trumpet" when the instrument can be named accurately. "Ram's horn blast" preserves both the instrument (*shofar*) and the type of sound (*teru'ah*).

**V. 10 — "Liberty" for deror.** All three agents render "liberty." Consensus: "liberty" — unanimous, and the ruling term per project terminology.

**V. 10 — "Each of you shall return to his property... his clan."** EMET: "each man to his holding and each man to his family." LOGOS: "each of you shall return to his property and each of you shall return to his clan." PNEUMA: "every one of you is to return to his own property; every one of you is to return to his own family." Consensus: LOGOS — "each of you shall return to his property and each of you shall return to his clan." "Clan" for *mishpachah* is the project convention in Jubilee legal contexts. PNEUMA's "family" and EMET's "family" are both acceptable alternatives; "clan" is preferred here for the legal-social specificity of *mishpachah* in this context.

**V. 11 — "Untended vines."** As per v. 5 ruling. LOGOS: "undressed vines." Consensus: "untended vines."

**V. 12 — "Produce of the field" vs. "what the fields produce on their own."** EMET: "from the field you may eat its produce." LOGOS: "You may eat the produce of the field." PNEUMA: "You may eat only what the fields produce on their own." Consensus: LOGOS — "You may eat the produce of the field." PNEUMA adds "only" and "on their own," importing a restriction not explicit in the MT at this point. LOGOS follows the MT *mi-sadeh tochlu et-tevu'atah* straightforwardly.

**V. 14 — "Sell anything" vs. "make a sale."** EMET: "when you sell a sale to your fellow." LOGOS: "if you make a sale to your neighbor." PNEUMA: "if you sell land to your neighbor." Consensus: "when you sell anything to your neighbor" — following the MT *ve-ki timkeru mimkar la-amitekha*. The Hebrew *mimkar* (a noun, "a sale/merchandise") is the object of the verb. Neither "a sale" (LOGOS) nor "land" (PNEUMA's addition) is ideal; "anything" follows EMET's sense without the Hebrew idiom awkwardness.

**V. 14 — "Neighbor" vs. "fellow."** EMET: "fellow" (*amitekha*). LOGOS/PNEUMA: "neighbor." Consensus: "neighbor" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. *Amith* means "fellow/associate/neighbor" and in the commercial context "neighbor" is the more natural English equivalent.

**V. 15 — Word order and structure.** EMET: "According to the number of years after the jubilee you shall buy from your fellow." LOGOS: "You shall pay your neighbor according to the number of years after the Jubilee, and he shall sell to you according to the number of years for crops." PNEUMA: "You are to buy from your neighbor according to the number of years since the last Jubilee, and he is to sell to you according to the number of remaining years of harvests." Consensus: LOGOS structure with minor adjustment — "According to the number of years after the Jubilee you shall buy from your neighbor, and according to the number of years for crops he shall sell to you." Preserves the Hebrew's two-clause structure and the symmetry of buying and selling.

**V. 17 — "Wrong one another" (repeated from v. 14).** All three agents agree. Consensus: "You shall not wrong one another" — unanimous.

**V. 18 — "Statutes and rules."** EMET: "my statutes and you shall keep my rules." LOGOS: "my statutes and keep my rules and perform them." PNEUMA: "my decrees... my laws." Consensus: "And you shall do my statutes and keep my rules and do them" — following LOGOS in rendering *chuqqotai* as "statutes" and *mishpatai* as "rules," consistent with the project convention from Lev 20 onward. PNEUMA's "decrees" and "laws" are acceptable alternatives but depart from the established terminology. The *vav* opening is preserved.

**V. 19 — "Eat your fill."** EMET: "and you shall eat to satisfaction." LOGOS: "you will eat your fill." PNEUMA: "you will eat your fill." Consensus: "eat your fill" — LOGOS/PNEUMA.

**V. 20 — "If you say."** EMET: "And if you say." LOGOS: "And if you say." PNEUMA: "You may wonder." Consensus: "And if you say" — EMET/LOGOS. The MT *ve-ki tomar'u* is a second-person plural indicative ("and if you [pl.] say"), not a hypothetical wondering. PNEUMA's paraphrase softens the directness of the anticipated objection.

**V. 21 — "Command my blessing."** EMET: "I will command my blessing upon you." LOGOS: "I will command my blessing on you." PNEUMA: "I will send my blessing." Consensus: "I will command my blessing on you" — LOGOS/EMET. The MT *ve-tsivviti et-birkhati* has the specific verb "to command" (*tsiwwah*), not "to send." YHWH commands his blessing as he commands his laws — the same verb used for divine decrees. PNEUMA's "send" loses this.

**V. 22 — "Eating from the old crop."** EMET: "you will eat from the old produce." LOGOS: "you will be eating some of the old crop." PNEUMA: "you will eat from the old harvest." Consensus: "you will be eating from the old crop; until the ninth year, when its crop arrives, you shall eat the old." This preserves the MT's structure which emphasizes continuing to eat from old stores across two years.

**V. 23 — "Sojourners and settlers" vs. "strangers and sojourners."** EMET: "for sojourners and settlers you are with me." LOGOS: "For you are strangers and sojourners with me." PNEUMA: "you are only strangers and sojourners living here with me." Consensus: "For you are sojourners and settlers with me." The MT has *gerim ve-toshavim* — *ger* (sojourner) and *toshav* (settler/resident alien). "Sojourners and settlers" follows EMET's distinction between the two terms and preserves the legal precision. LOGOS's "strangers and sojourners" uses two near-synonyms for different Hebrew words. PNEUMA's "only strangers and sojourners" adds "only," not in the MT. The preposition "with me" (*immadi*) is retained — God dwells in the land alongside Israel, making Israel tenants in God's own residence.

**V. 24 — "Land of your holding."** EMET: "throughout all the land of your holding." LOGOS: "in all the country you possess." PNEUMA: "throughout the land that you hold." Consensus: "throughout all the land of your holding" — EMET. The MT has *u-ve-khol eretz achuzatekhem*, where *achuzah* is the technical term for ancestral land-holding. "Holding" preserves this technical sense. "Country" (LOGOS) is too broad; "hold" (PNEUMA, verbal) converts the noun.

**V. 25 — "Nearest kinsman-redeemer."** EMET: "his redeemer [go'alo], the nearest one." LOGOS: "his nearest redeemer." PNEUMA: "his nearest kinsman-redeemer." Consensus: "his nearest kinsman-redeemer" — PNEUMA. The full compound "kinsman-redeemer" (per project terminology ruling) is preferred over bare "redeemer" in this verse because this is the first introduction of the *go'el* in the chapter and the familial aspect must be established. Subsequent uses in the same paragraph may use "redeemer" for brevity.

**V. 26 — "Becomes prosperous."** EMET: "his hand attains enough." LOGOS: "himself becomes prosperous and finds sufficient means." PNEUMA: "he himself later prospers enough." Consensus: "he himself becomes prosperous and finds sufficient means" — LOGOS. The MT has *ve-hissiggah yado* ("and his hand attains") — the idiom means acquiring sufficient resources. LOGOS's paraphrase captures both the prosperity and the sufficiency clearly. EMET's literal "his hand attains" is obscure in English.

**V. 27 — "Repay the balance."** EMET: "return the excess to the man." LOGOS: "pay back the balance to the man." PNEUMA: "refund the difference to the man." Consensus: "repay the balance to the man" — LOGOS. The MT *ve-shillem et-ha'odeph la-ish* = "and he shall pay back the surplus/excess to the man." "Balance" and "difference" (PNEUMA) are both acceptable; "balance" (LOGOS) is the most natural financial term.

**V. 28 — "Released" for redemption at Jubilee.** EMET: "it shall go out [ve-yatsa] in the jubilee." LOGOS: "it shall be released in the Jubilee." PNEUMA: "It will be returned in the Jubilee." Consensus: "it shall be released in the Jubilee" — LOGOS. *Yatsa* ("to go out/depart") is the Exodus verb, but for land the conventional translation is "released" (cf. property law contexts). "Returned" (PNEUMA) implies something slightly different. "Released" follows established usage.

**V. 29 — "Dwelling house in a walled city."** EMET: "a house of dwelling." LOGOS/PNEUMA: "a dwelling house." Consensus: "a dwelling house in a walled city" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. *Beit moshav* = "house of residence/dwelling" — "dwelling house" is the natural English compound.

**V. 30 — "Pass in perpetuity" vs. "made permanent property."** EMET: "shall be established in perpetuity." LOGOS: "shall be made permanent property of the buyer." PNEUMA: "becomes the permanent possession of the buyer." Consensus: "shall pass in perpetuity to the buyer throughout his generations" — synthesizing EMET's "perpetuity" with the subject of the transfer. The MT *ve-qam ha-bayit... li-tsemitut la-qoneh le-dorotav* = "and the house shall rise/stand in perpetuity to the buyer throughout his generations." "Pass in perpetuity" captures *qam* (the standing/establishing of title) and *li-tsemitut* (permanently).

**V. 31 — "Villages that have no surrounding wall."** EMET: "villages that have no wall surrounding them." LOGOS: "villages that have no surrounding wall." PNEUMA: "villages without surrounding walls." Consensus: LOGOS — "villages that have no surrounding wall." Precise and natural.

**V. 31 — "Classified with the open fields."** EMET: "shall be reckoned upon the open land of the field." LOGOS: "shall be classified with the fields of the land." PNEUMA: "are to be treated like open fields." Consensus: "shall be classified with the open fields of the land" — adapting LOGOS with "open" from EMET's *migrash* sense. The MT *al sdeh ha-aretz yechashev* = "shall be reckoned as the open field of the land."

**V. 32 — "Permanent right of redemption."** EMET: "shall have a perpetual right of redemption." LOGOS: "may redeem at any time." PNEUMA: "always retain the right to redeem." Consensus: "shall have a permanent right of redemption" — a synthesis. The MT *ge'ulat olam* = "perpetual/eternal redemption" — this is an established legal right, permanent and unrestricted. "Permanent right of redemption" captures *olam* (permanent) and *ge'ulah* (right of redemption).

**V. 33 — "Holding among the sons of Israel."** EMET: "their holding among the sons of Yisra'el." LOGOS: "their possession among the sons of Israel." PNEUMA: "their holding among the Israelites." Consensus: "their holding among the sons of Israel" — EMET/adjusted. "Holding" for *achuzah* is the project term; "sons of Israel" per project convention over PNEUMA's "Israelites."

**V. 34 — "Open land" / "permanent holding."** EMET: "the open land of their cities." LOGOS: "the fields of pastureland belonging to their cities." PNEUMA: "the pastureland belonging to their towns." Consensus: "the open land belonging to their cities" — following the MT *sdeh migrash arehhem*, where *migrash* = open land/commons/pastureland outside city walls. "Open land" preserves the term; "pastureland" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is an interpretation of the use rather than the term itself. "Permanent holding" for *achuzat olam* — per project *achuzah* convention.

**V. 35 — "Cannot support himself with you."** EMET: "and his hand falters beside you." LOGOS: "cannot maintain himself with you." PNEUMA: "cannot support himself among you." Consensus: "and cannot support himself with you" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. The MT *u-matah yado immakh* = "and his hand sinks/totters with you." The idiom means inability to sustain himself. LOGOS's "maintain himself" and PNEUMA's "support himself" are both accurate; "support himself" is slightly more natural. "With you" (*immakh*) is retained.

**V. 35 — "As a sojourner and a settler."** EMET: "as a sojourner and a settler." LOGOS: "as though he were a sojourner and a resident alien." PNEUMA: "as you would a foreigner or sojourner." Consensus: "as a sojourner and a settler" — EMET. The MT *ke-ger ve-toshav* = "as a sojourner and a settler." LOGOS's "as though he were" introduces a comparative distancing not in the Hebrew; the instruction is direct. PNEUMA's reversal of the terms and use of "foreigner" departs from the established pairing. "Sojourner" for *ger* and "settler" for *toshav* per project convention.

**V. 36 — "Interest or profit."** EMET: "interest or profit" (*neshekh ve-tarbit*). LOGOS: "interest... or profit." PNEUMA: "interest of any kind." Consensus: "interest or profit" — EMET/LOGOS. The MT has two distinct terms: *neshekh* (interest/bite, typically on money) and *tarbit* (profit/increase, typically on goods). Both must be preserved. PNEUMA's "interest of any kind" collapses the distinction.

**V. 37 — "Money at interest... food for profit."** EMET: "your money... at interest [be-neshekh]... your food for profit [be-tarbit]." LOGOS: same structure. PNEUMA: "money at interest... food for profit." All three agents agree on the substance. Consensus: LOGOS/EMET — "You shall not lend him your money at interest, nor give him your food for profit."

**V. 39 — "Make him serve as a slave."** EMET: "you shall not work with him the work of a slave." LOGOS: "you shall not make him serve as a slave." PNEUMA: "do not make him work as a slave." Consensus: LOGOS — "you shall not make him serve as a slave." The MT *lo ta'avod bo avodat eved* = "you shall not work him with the work of a slave." LOGOS renders the sense accurately and idiomatically.

**V. 40 — "Settler" for toshav.** EMET: "like a settler." LOGOS: "as a sojourner." PNEUMA: "as a sojourner living with you." Consensus: "as a hired worker and as a settler" — EMET. The MT has *ke-sakhir ke-toshav yihyeh immakh* — "as a hired worker, as a settler he shall be with you." LOGOS uses "sojourner" for *toshav* here, departing from the pairing established at v. 35. Consistency demands "settler" for *toshav* throughout. EMET is correct.

**V. 41 — "Return to his own clan."** EMET: "to his family." LOGOS: "to his own clan." PNEUMA: "to his own family." Consensus: "to his own clan" — LOGOS. Consistent with *mishpachah* = "clan" in Jubilee legal sections (cf. v. 10 ruling).

**V. 43 — "Ruthlessly" for be-farekh.** All three agents agree: "ruthlessly." Consensus: "ruthlessly" — unanimous. Applied consistently at vv. 43, 46, 53.

**V. 44 — "Nations that are around you."** EMET: "from the nations that are around you." LOGOS: same. PNEUMA: "the nations around you." Consensus: LOGOS — "from among the nations that are around you." The *me'et* ("from among") is preserved.

**V. 45 — "Sons of the settlers."** EMET: "from the sons of the settlers." LOGOS: "from among the sojourners... and from their families." PNEUMA: "from the foreigners residing among you." Consensus: "from among the sons of the settlers who are temporary residents among you and from their families that are with you, who have been born in your land" — following the MT's detail and LOGOS's structure, but with "settlers" for *toshavim* per project convention.

**V. 46 — "Permanent holding" / "bequeath."** EMET: "you shall cause them to be inherited by your sons." LOGOS: "bequeath them to your sons." PNEUMA: "will them to your children." Consensus: "You may bequeath them to your sons after you to inherit as a permanent holding" — LOGOS adjusted. The MT *ve-hithnachaltem otam li-vneikhem achreikhim la-reshet achuzah le-olam* = "you shall cause them to be inherited by your sons after you to possess as a perpetual holding." "Permanent holding" for *achuzat olam* per project convention.

**V. 47 — "Sojourner or settler with you becomes prosperous."** EMET: "the hand of a sojourner or settler attains enough." LOGOS: "a sojourner or a resident alien with you becomes rich." PNEUMA: "a foreigner or a sojourner living among you becomes wealthy." Consensus: "a sojourner or settler with you becomes prosperous" — following project *ger*/*toshav* terminology and replacing "rich" with "prosperous" to match v. 26's language for the same economic shift.

**V. 48 — "Right of redemption."** EMET: "redemption shall remain for him." LOGOS: "he may be redeemed." PNEUMA: "he retains the right of redemption." Consensus: "he retains the right of redemption" — PNEUMA. The MT *ge'ulah tihyeh lo* = "redemption shall be to him/he shall have redemption." PNEUMA's "retains the right of redemption" captures the legal entitlement clearly.

**V. 49 — "Uncle's son" = "cousin."** EMET: "his uncle or his uncle's son." LOGOS: "his uncle or his cousin." PNEUMA: "his uncle or his cousin." Consensus: LOGOS/PNEUMA — "his uncle or his uncle's son." Wait — LOGOS renders *ben-dodo* as "cousin" and EMET renders it literally as "his uncle's son." Consensus: "his uncle or his uncle's son" — EMET. The literal *ben-dodo* ("son of his uncle") is more transparent than "cousin" in a legal kinship context where the exact degree of relationship matters.

**V. 50 — "Reckoned as the time of a hired worker."** EMET: "like the days of a hired worker it shall be with him." LOGOS: "the time he was with his buyer shall be rated as the time of a hired worker." PNEUMA: "based on the rate paid to a hired hand for that number of years." Consensus: "The time he was with his buyer shall be reckoned as the time of a hired worker" — LOGOS adjusted. "Reckoned" over "rated" for *yihyeh* ("shall be").

**V. 51 — "Proportionately" / "in proportion to them."** EMET: "in proportion to them he shall return his redemption price." LOGOS: "he shall pay proportionately for his redemption some of his sale price." PNEUMA: "he must repay a larger share of the price he was purchased for." Consensus: "in proportion to them he shall return his redemption price from the sum he was purchased for" — EMET/adjusted. Preserves the proportionality language while making the direction of payment clear.

**V. 53 — "Hired worker year by year."** EMET: "like a hired worker year by year he shall be with him." LOGOS: "He shall treat him as a worker hired year by year." PNEUMA: "He is to be treated as a hired hand year by year." Consensus: "He shall be with him as a hired worker year by year" — EMET/adjusted. The MT *ki-sakhir shanah be-shanah yihyeh immo* = "as a hired worker year by year he shall be with him." This preserves "hired worker" (*sakhir*) over LOGOS's "worker" and keeps the subject consistent (the enslaved man, not the master).

**V. 54 — "Go out" vs. "be released."** EMET: "he shall go out [ve-yatsa]." LOGOS: "shall be released." PNEUMA: "are to be released." Consensus: "shall go out" — EMET. The verb *yatsa* ("to go out/depart") is the Exodus liberation verb used throughout Lev 25 (cf. v. 41). At v. 28 "released" was used for property; here for persons the Exodus-resonant "go out" is appropriate and intentional. This is EMET's most important lexical contribution to the chapter.

**V. 55 — "For it is to me that the sons of Israel are servants."** EMET: "For to me the sons of Yisra'el are servants." LOGOS: "For it is to me that the sons of Israel are servants." PNEUMA: "For the Israelites belong to me as servants." Consensus: LOGOS — "For it is to me that the sons of Israel are servants." The MT *ki-li benei Yisra'el avadim* = "for to me the sons of Israel are servants." LOGOS's fronted "to me" (*ki-li*) preserves the emphatic *ki-li* echo of v. 23 (*ki-li ha-aretz*). PNEUMA's "belong to me" softens the servant-language. "Sons of Israel" per project convention.

---

## Textual Notes for the Consensus Record

**On the ki-li inclusio (vv. 23, 55):** The chapter is structurally bracketed by two *ki-li* ("for mine") declarations: *ki-li ha-aretz* (v. 23, "for the land is mine") and *ki-li benei Yisra'el avadim* (v. 55, "for to me the sons of Israel are servants"). The consensus preserves the verbal parallel of "for the land is mine" and "for it is to me that the sons of Israel are servants" — both fronting the divine claim with *ki-li*. This architectural feature is the chapter's governing theological framework.

**On yatsa as the Exodus verb (vv. 28, 41, 54, 55):** The verb *yatsa* ("to go out") is used consistently for the release of enslaved Israelites and alienated land at Jubilee. It is the same root as "bringing out" (*hotsethi*) in the Exodus formula of vv. 38, 42, and 55. The consensus renders *yatsa* as "go out" for persons (vv. 41, 54) and "released" for property (v. 28), while preserving the Exodus echo in the final verses.

**On achuzah (vv. 10, 13, 24–28, 33–34, 41, 45–46):** The term *achuzah* (ancestral land-holding, from *achaz*, "to grasp/hold") is the chapter's technical term for the inalienable patrimonial share of land. The consensus renders it "property" in most verses (following common English usage in legal contexts) and "holding" when the emphasis is on the legal-ancestral character (vv. 24, 33–34, 46). EMET uses "holding" throughout with greater consistency; LOGOS uses "possession" and PNEUMA uses various terms. The disagreement log records all instances.

**On the graduated poverty scale (vv. 25, 35, 39, 47):** The verb *yamukh* ("to become poor/sink") opens four successive legal cases: (1) selling land (v. 25), (2) requiring personal support (v. 35), (3) selling oneself to an Israelite (v. 39), (4) selling oneself to a foreigner (v. 47). This is a descending scale of economic catastrophe with corresponding remedies at each level. The consensus renders *yamukh* as "becomes poor" throughout.

**On be-farekh (vv. 43, 46, 53):** The term *be-farekh* ("with crushing force/ruthlessly") appears only in Exod 1:13–14 (of Egypt's treatment of Israel) and these three verses. The Exodus echo is deliberate: the prohibition on *be-farekh* treatment of enslaved Israelites is grounded in Israel's own experience of *be-farekh* under Pharaoh. The consensus renders it "ruthlessly" throughout, following the established project convention.

**On LXX Vaticanus (pp. 111–112):** Notable LXX features: (1) *yovel* is rendered variously as *aphesis* ("release/remission") and *iōbēl* (phonetic transliteration) — the same word *aphesis* used for forgiveness of sins in the New Testament; (2) v. 23's *li-tsemitut* ("in perpetuity") is rendered *eis bebaiōsin* ("for permanent confirmation/guarantee") — capturing the legal irrevocability; (3) v. 10's *deror* is rendered *aphesis* — connecting the Jubilee directly to Isaiah 61:1 and Luke 4:18 in the Greek tradition; (4) v. 47's *ger* is rendered *prosēlytos* ("proselyte") rather than *paroikos* ("sojourner"), reflecting a later narrowing of the term.

---

*SOPHIA consensus completed. Leviticus 25:1–55. All 55 verses rendered. Source arbiter rulings logged in `disagreements/LEV_025.md`.*
